overview: This area addresses the ethical, legal, and regulatory dimensions of AI use in HEIs. Within the Alfa Labs project, it ensures that the deployment of AI tools in teaching, learning, administration, and research is aligned with EU-level and national legislation (notably the EU AI Act and GDPR) as well as with institutional values of fairness, transparency, accountability, and academic integrity. Final Beneficiary Report For universities, adopting AI responsibly is a prerequisite to building trust among students, faculty, and the public. While AI can deliver efficiency, personalization, and innovation, if misused it can lead to bias, breaches of privacy, or reputational damage. The Alfa Labs approach emphasizes that responsible AI governance is not a barrier but an enabler of sustainable digital transformation. This area covers: 1. Raising awareness of EU AI Act obligations and ethical guidelines. 2. Developing clear institutional policies for ethical AI use in teaching, learning, and research. 3. Ensuring data protection compliance adapted for AI (GDPR + AI-specific safeguards). 4. Transparent procurement and vendor accountability. 5. Establishing ethics and compliance committees to monitor AI adoption. 6. Training of staff and students in responsible AI practices. 7. Continuous monitoring and auditing of risks such as bias, discrimination, and misuse.

instructions.maturity_scoring: For each item/statement in the following table, select: [0] Not in place | [1] Partially in place | [2] Fully in place, providing also clear evidence for the suggested score and owner responsible for this item. Examples for evidence and owner are provided within the table below. Note that items which are marked as Critical (with red dot) need to be implemented before approaching to higher maturity level (necessary/minimum conditions).

items.item: Awareness of EU AI Act among leadership and staff

score_anchors.2: Ongoing awareness campaigns and staff training

items.evidence_examples: Records of workshops, attendance lists, training materials

items.item: Official guidelines for ethical AI use
Data protection policies adapted for AI applications (GDPR+)
Transparent and accountable AI procurement processes
Ethics or compliance committee overseeing AI deployment
Staff and student training in responsible AI use
items.owners: HR Departme nt, Student Parliamen t

items.item: Active monitoring of risks (bias, misuse, discrimination)
items.owners: University Ethics Committe e, Center for Informati on Systems

score_ranges.interpretation: Awareness of ethical and legal AI aspects is low or fragmented. No formal policies, committees, or training in place. Risk management is reactive.

Basic ethics and compliance structures exist (e.g., guidelines, training, partial GDPR adaptation). Governance is in development but not yet fully embedded institution-wide. Monitoring and accountability are still ad hoc.

Ethical AI principles are fully institutionalised. Comprehensive governance, regular risk audits, transparent procurement, and continuous training ensure compliance with the EU AI Act and GDPR+. The institution demonstrates leadership in responsible AI.

build_action_plan.intro: Before approaching a higher level of maturity in the AI regulations and ethics DTP area, the critical items from 3.8.2 need to be fully implemented. For each unmet critical item, the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table of foundational actions below).

foundational_actions.action: Conduct awareness sessions on EU AI Act & GDPR for leadership and staff.
Draft and formally adopt institutional guidelines for ethical AI use.
Update and monitor data protection policies to address AI-specific risks (GDPR+).
Ensure transparent and auditable AI procurement processes.
Establish an ethics or AI governance committee with a formal mandate to oversee AI adoption, including regular meetings and decision-making procedures.
Introduce a basic AI risk register and schedule at least annual reviews of AI systems for bias, misuse, and discrimination, with documented mitigation steps
foundational_actions.owners: University Ethics Committee, Center for Information Systems

Emerging.action: Conduct awareness sessions on EU AI Act & GDPR.
Draft ethical AI guidelines for teaching, research, and administration.
Establish an interdisciplinary AI Ethics Working Group.
Identify high-risk AI applications as defined by the AI Act.

Established.action: Formalize AI ethics audits across institutional systems.
Established.owners: University Ethics Committee, Center for Information Systems

Established.action: Apply ethics-by-design in procurement and development.
Establish a transparency register of institutional AI systems.
Run mandatory staff and student training on bias, risks, and responsible use.

Enhanced.action: Integrate full AI Act compliance and ethical governance into institutional policy cycles, with annual reviews and reporting to leadership.
Enhanced.owners: Legal and Compliance Office, University Ethics Committee

Enhanced.action: Operationalize a permanent AI Governance Council that oversees ethics, risk management, and compliance across all faculties and administrative units.
Launch continuous training programs in responsible and transparent AI use for staff and students, embedded in professional development and curricula.
Implement systematic AI risk monitoring and auditing, including bias detection, model transparency, and accountability reporting.
Enhanced.owners: Center for Information Systems, University Ethics Committee

Enhanced.action: Ensure AI procurement follows ethics-by-design principles, with transparent vendor assessments and published audit results.
Maintain a public AI ethics and compliance register, summarizing institutional AI systems, risk categories, and mitigation actions.

kpis.intro: The following table presents key performance indicators (KPIs) for this area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: systems reviewed under ethics & compliance frameworks (annual)
items.owners: University Ethics Committee, Center for Information Systems

items.name: incidents of AI-related bias/misuse detected per year
items.owners: University Ethics Committee, Center for Information Systems
